---
title: "Week 4 Lab: String Mastery and Text Processing"
subtitle: "Hands-on Practice with Slicing, Methods, and Data Transformation"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    embed-resources: true
    self-contained: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
---

# Lab Overview

This lab will help you master string operations through hands-on practice. You'll learn to slice strings precisely, apply methods effectively, and build your first text-based game.

**Duration:** 2 hours  
**Goals:**
1. Master string and list slicing notation
2. Apply string methods for data cleaning
3. Parse and transform structured text
4. Build engaging word games
5. Handle edge cases gracefully

---

# Hour 1: Slicing and String Methods

## Part 1: String Slicing Mastery (20 minutes)

### Exercise 1.1: Basic Slicing

Given the string below, extract the requested parts:

```python
text = "Python Programming"
#       012345678901234567  # Helper: indices
```

Extract:
1. First word: `_____[___:___]`  → "Python"
2. Second word: `_____[___:___]` → "Programming"
3. First 3 characters: `_____[___:___]` → "Pyt"
4. Last 4 characters: `_____[___:___]` → "ming"
5. Middle 5 characters: `_____[___:___]` → "on Pr"

### Exercise 1.2: Negative Indexing

```python
email = "student@university.edu"
#       0         1         2
#       012345678901234567890  # Forward
#                            -1 # Backward
```

Extract using negative indices:
1. Domain (after @): `email[___:]`
2. Username (before @): `email[:___]`
3. Extension (.edu): `email[___:]`
4. Without extension: `email[:___]`

### Exercise 1.3: Step Parameter

```python
alphabet = "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ"
```

Create:
1. Every 2nd letter: `alphabet[::___]` → "ACEGIKMOQSUWY"
2. Every 3rd letter: `alphabet[::___]` → "ADGJMPSVY"
3. Reversed string: `alphabet[___]` → "ZYXWVUTSRQPONMLKJIHGFEDCBA"
4. Every 2nd letter, backwards: `alphabet[___]`

### Exercise 1.4: Practical Slicing

Complete these real-world slicing tasks:

```python
# Extract parts from filename
filename = "report_2024_final.pdf"
name = filename[___:___]        # "report_2024_final"
extension = filename[___:]      # "pdf"
year = filename[___:___]        # "2024"

# Extract from phone number
phone = "(555) 123-4567"
area_code = phone[___:___]      # "555"
first_part = phone[___:___]     # "123"
last_part = phone[___:]         # "4567"

# Extract from URL
url = "https://www.example.com/page"
protocol = url[___:___]         # "https"
domain = ___                    # Hint: use find() first
```

## Part 2: String Methods Practice (20 minutes)

### Exercise 2.1: Case Methods

```python
# Fix these names
name1 = "john doe"
name2 = "JANE SMITH"
name3 = "mArY jOhNsOn"

# Your code:
clean1 = name1._____()  # "John Doe"
clean2 = name2._____()._____()  # "Jane Smith"
clean3 = name3._____()._____()  # "Mary Johnson"

# Case-insensitive comparison
user_input = "YeS"
if user_input._____() == "yes":
    print("User agreed!")
```

### Exercise 2.2: Whitespace Cleaning

```python
# Clean these messy inputs
input1 = "  hello  "
input2 = "\tPython\n"
input3 = "  multiple   spaces  here  "

# Your code:
clean1 = input1._____()  # "hello"
clean2 = input2._____()  # "Python"

# For input3, remove extra spaces
clean3 = input3.strip()
# Now remove multiple spaces - complete this:
while "  " in clean3:
    clean3 = clean3._____("  ", " ")
```

### Exercise 2.3: Search and Replace

```python
text = "I love programming in Java. Java is great!"

# Tasks:
# 1. Check if "Java" is in the text
if _____ in text:
    print("Found Java")

# 2. Find position of first "Java"
pos = text._____("Java")
print(f"First Java at position: {pos}")

# 3. Count occurrences
count = text._____("Java")
print(f"Java appears {count} times")

# 4. Replace all occurrences
python_text = text._____("Java", "Python")
print(python_text)

# 5. Replace only first occurrence
first_only = text._____("Java", "Python", ___)
```

### Exercise 2.4: Method Chaining

Complete these method chains:

```python
# Clean and standardize email
email = "  USER@GMAIL.COM  "
clean_email = email._____()._____()  # "user@gmail.com"

# Clean and format name
name = "  john    doe  "
# Remove extra spaces and proper case
clean_name = " ".join(name._____()._____())._____()

# Extract and clean domain
messy_email = "  Student@UNIVERSITY.EDU  \n"
domain = messy_email._____()._____(___)._____(___)[___]._____()
# Should give: "university.edu"
```

## Part 3: Split and Join Operations (20 minutes)

### Exercise 3.1: Basic Splitting

```python
# Split these strings appropriately
csv_data = "John,25,Engineer"
fields = csv_data._____(",")
name = fields[___]
age = fields[___]
job = fields[___]

# Split on different delimiters
phone = "555-123-4567"
parts = phone._____(___)  # Get list of three parts

# Split with limit
text = "one-two-three-four-five"
first_three = text._____("-", ___)  # ["one", "two", "three-four-five"]

# Split on whitespace
sentence = "  Python   is    awesome  "
words = sentence._____()  # Automatically handles multiple spaces!
```

### Exercise 3.2: Joining Lists

```python
# Join these lists into strings
words = ["Python", "is", "fun"]
sentence = _____.join(words)  # "Python is fun"

# Create different formats
items = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
comma_list = _____.join(items)  # "apple,banana,cherry"
nice_list = _____.join(items)   # "apple, banana, cherry"
bullet_list = _____.join(items)  # "apple\n- banana\n- cherry"

# Build a formatted message
data = ["John", "25", "Engineer"]
message = "Name: {}, Age: {}, Job: {}".format(data[0], data[1], data[2])
# Or using join:
formatted = _____.join([f"Name: {data[0]}", f"Age: {data[1]}", f"Job: {data[2]}"])
```

### Exercise 3.3: Parse and Rebuild

```python
# Parse email list and clean each one
email_string = "john@GMAIL.com; ALICE@yahoo.COM;bob@HOTMAIL.com"

# Step 1: Split into list
emails = email_string.split(_____)

# Step 2: Clean each email
clean_emails = []
for email in emails:
    clean = email._____._____ 
    clean_emails.append(clean)

# Step 3: Join back
clean_string = _____.join(clean_emails)
print(clean_string)  # "john@gmail.com; alice@yahoo.com; bob@hotmail.com"
```

---

# Hour 2: Integration and Word Game

## Part 4: Data Transformation Patterns (20 minutes)

### Exercise 4.1: Input Validation

Create a username validator:

```python
# Username rules:
# - 3-20 characters
# - Only letters, numbers, underscore
# - Must start with letter
# - Lowercase only

username = input("Choose username: ").strip().lower()

# Check length
if len(username) < 3:
    print("Too short!")
elif len(username) > 20:
    print("Too long!")
# Check first character
elif not username[0]._____():  # Check if letter
    print("Must start with letter!")
# Check all characters
else:
    valid = True
    for char in username:
        if not (char._____ or char._____ or char == "_"):
            valid = False
            break
    
    if valid:
        print(f"Username '{username}' is valid!")
    else:
        print("Invalid characters!")
```

### Exercise 4.2: Text Statistics

Analyze a paragraph:

```python
text = """Python is a great language.
It is easy to learn and powerful.
Many people love Python."""

# Calculate statistics
# 1. Word count
words = text.replace("\n", " ").split()
word_count = _____

# 2. Sentence count (periods)
sentence_count = text._____(".")

# 3. Average word length
total_length = 0
for word in words:
    total_length = total_length + len(word)
avg_length = _____ / _____

# 4. Most common word length
length_counts = {}  # We'll learn dictionaries later
# For now, just find the longest word
longest = ""
for word in words:
    if len(word) > len(longest):
        longest = word

print(f"Words: {word_count}")
print(f"Sentences: {sentence_count}")
print(f"Avg word length: {avg_length:.1f}")
print(f"Longest word: {longest}")
```

### Exercise 4.3: CSV Processing

Parse student data:

```python
# CSV data with header
csv_data = """Name,Age,Grade
Alice Smith,20,A
Bob Jones,19,B
Charlie Brown,21,A
Diana Lee,20,C"""

# Split into lines
lines = csv_data.strip().split("\n")

# Skip header
header = lines[0]
data_lines = lines[1:]

# Process each student
students = []
for line in data_lines:
    parts = line.split(",")
    name = parts[0]
    age = int(parts[1])
    grade = parts[2]
    
    # Create formatted string
    student_info = f"{name} (Age: {age}) - Grade: {grade}"
    students.append(student_info)

# Display results
print("Student Report:")
for student in students:
    print(f"- {student}")
```

## Part 5: Word Game Project Start (40 minutes)

### Choose Your Game

Pick ONE of these games to implement:

#### Option 1: Word Scrambler
```python
# Game: Scramble a word, player guesses original
# Features needed:
# - Scramble letters randomly (tricky without imports!)
# - Check if guess matches original
# - Give hints (first letter, length)
# - Score tracking
```

#### Option 2: Simple Hangman
```python
# Game: Guess letters in a word
# Features needed:
# - Hide word with underscores
# - Reveal correct guesses
# - Track wrong guesses
# - Limited attempts
```

#### Option 3: Word Chain
```python
# Game: Each word must start with last letter of previous
# Features needed:
# - Check if word starts correctly
# - Prevent repeated words
# - Score based on word length
# - Show valid next letters
```

### Starter Code: Word Scrambler

```python
print("=== Word Scrambler Game ===\n")

# Word list (add more!)
words = ["python", "program", "computer", "science", "coding"]

# Game variables
score = 0
round_num = 1
playing = True

while playing and round_num <= len(words):
    # Get word for this round
    original = words[round_num - 1]
    
    # Simple scramble (reverse for now)
    scrambled = original[::-1]
    
    print(f"\nRound {round_num}")
    print(f"Scrambled word: {scrambled}")
    print(f"Hint: {len(original)} letters, starts with '{original[0]}'")
    
    # Get guess
    attempts = 3
    guessed = False
    
    while attempts > 0 and not guessed:
        guess = input(f"\nYour guess ({attempts} left): ").strip().lower()
        
        if guess == original:
            print("Correct! +10 points")
            score = score + 10
            guessed = True
        else:
            attempts = attempts - 1
            if attempts > 0:
                print("Wrong! Try again.")
                # Give another hint
                if attempts == 1:
                    print(f"Hint: ends with '{original[-1]}'")
    
    if not guessed:
        print(f"Out of attempts! Word was: {original}")
    
    # Next round?
    round_num = round_num + 1
    if round_num > len(words):
        print(f"\nGame Over! Final score: {score}")
        playing = False
    else:
        continue_game = input("\nContinue? (y/n): ").lower()
        if continue_game != 'y':
            playing = False

print(f"\nThanks for playing! Final score: {score}")
```

### Game Enhancement Ideas

1. **Better Scrambling** (without random module):
   ```python
   # Swap adjacent pairs
   scrambled = ""
   for i in range(0, len(word)-1, 2):
       scrambled = scrambled + word[i+1] + word[i]
   if len(word) % 2 == 1:
       scrambled = scrambled + word[-1]
   ```

2. **Difficulty Levels**:
   - Easy: Show first and last letter
   - Medium: Show only first letter
   - Hard: No hints

3. **Score System**:
   - Base points for correct answer
   - Bonus for speed (fewer attempts)
   - Streak bonus

4. **Word Categories**:
   - Animals, foods, countries
   - Let player choose category

---

# Lab Submission

## What to Submit

1. **Completed Exercises** from Parts 1-4 in one Python file
2. **Word Game** implementation (as complete as possible)
3. **Test Cases** showing your game handles:
   - Valid input
   - Invalid input  
   - Edge cases
4. **Reflection** (3-5 sentences):
   - Which string operation was most challenging?
   - How did you handle edge cases?
   - What would you add to your game?

## Grading Rubric

| Component | Points | Criteria |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| Slicing Exercises | 20 | Correct slice notation |
| String Methods | 20 | Appropriate method usage |
| Split/Join | 15 | Proper parsing and rebuilding |
| Data Transformation | 15 | Clean, working solutions |
| Word Game | 20 | Core features work |
| Code Quality | 10 | Clear, well-organized |

---

# Common Issues and Solutions

## Slicing Errors

**Problem:**
```python
text = "Hello"
last_two = text[-2]  # Gets one character!
```

**Solution:** Remember slicing needs start:end
```python
last_two = text[-2:]  # Gets "lo"
```

## String Immutability

**Problem:**
```python
word = "test"
word[0] = "T"  # Error!
```

**Solution:** Build new string
```python
word = "T" + word[1:]  # "Test"
```

## Split Edge Cases

**Problem:**
```python
"a,,b".split(",")  # ['a', '', 'b'] - empty string!
```

**Solution:** Filter empty strings
```python
parts = [p for p in text.split(",") if p]  # Advanced!
# Or with loop:
clean_parts = []
for part in text.split(","):
    if part:  # Not empty
        clean_parts.append(part)
```

## Method Order Matters

**Problem:**
```python
"  HELLO  ".lower().strip()  # "  hello  "
```

**Solution:** Strip first, then transform
```python
"  HELLO  ".strip().lower()  # "hello"
```

---

# Extra Challenges

If you finish early:

1. **Advanced Scrambling**: Mix letters better without random
2. **Two-Player Mode**: Take turns guessing
3. **Timer Feature**: Track solving time
4. **Word Builder**: Make words from given letters
5. **Pattern Matcher**: Find words matching pattern like "C_T" → "CAT"

---

# AI Usage Guidelines

## Good Prompts for Learning

✅ "Explain why string slicing uses exclusive end index"  
✅ "Show me how split() handles multiple delimiters"  
✅ "What's the difference between strip() and replace(' ', '')?"  
✅ "How can I reverse a string without [::-1]?"  

## Avoid These Prompts

❌ "Write my word game for me"  
❌ "Give me the complete solution"  
❌ "How do I use regex for this?"  

Remember: We're learning fundamentals, not shortcuts!

---

# Next Steps

After completing this lab:
1. Practice slicing with different strings
2. Try combining multiple string methods
3. Enhance your word game
4. Think about text patterns you encounter daily

Next week: Functions to organize our code better!
